findings.title: No authentication or authorization on any API endpoint
findings.description: Every endpoint — including sensitive write operations like /sync/import, /playbooks/generate/{cluster_id}, /feedback, and /analysis-log — is publicly accessible with no authentication mechanism. There are no FastAPI Depends() guards, no API key validation, no session tokens, and no role-based access control anywhere in the codebase. The sync comment at line 373 of sync.py acknowledges this: 'SEC-013: This endpoint modifies the knowledge base. In production it should be protected by an admin role or API key (e.g. via a FastAPI dependency). Currently relies on network-level access controls (internal only).' This design depends entirely on network perimeter security, which is insufficient for production.
findings.impact: Any user who can reach the API can: (1) trigger expensive LLM calls via /analyze, (2) import arbitrary data into the knowledge base via /sync/import, (3) regenerate all playbooks via /playbooks/generate/{id}, (4) access all incident data, resolution notes, and analysis logs, (5) read internal system stats via /health/detailed. Rate limiting exists on /analyze and /sync but not on data-read endpoints.
findings.evidence: backend/api/routers/sync.py:373 — explicit comment acknowledging the missing auth. All routers lack HTTPBearer, APIKeyHeader, or OAuth2PasswordBearer dependencies.
findings.recommendation:
Implement at minimum API key authentication using FastAPI's HTTPBearer or APIKeyHeader dependency. Apply it globally via a router-level dependency or individually on sensitive mutation endpoints. Example:

from fastapi.security import APIKeyHeader
api_key_header = APIKeyHeader(name='X-API-Key')

async def verify_api_key(api_key: str = Depends(api_key_header)):
    if api_key != os.getenv('REXUS_API_KEY'):
        raise HTTPException(403, 'Invalid API key')

# Apply to router:
router = APIRouter(dependencies=[Depends(verify_api_key)])

findings.title: /health/detailed endpoint exposes sensitive infrastructure information without authentication
findings.description: The /health/detailed endpoint (unauthenticated, publicly accessible) returns: database connection pool sizing and connection counts, LLM provider configuration (model names, provider type, AWS region), ServiceNow instance hostname, rate limit configuration, application version, environment name (development/production), uptime data, API call counts, estimated API costs, and analysis activity metrics. None of this data should be visible to unauthenticated callers.
findings.impact: An attacker gains a complete picture of the backend infrastructure: which LLM provider, which AWS region, which ServiceNow instance domain, how busy the system is, and the exact rate limits to probe under. This is a high-value reconnaissance endpoint.
findings.evidence: backend/api/routers/health.py:119-128 — returns ServiceNow instance domain. Lines 97-101 — returns LLM provider + model. Lines 177-181 — returns rate limit values. No auth dependency on the router.
findings.recommendation: Either gate /health/detailed behind authentication or strip all sensitive fields and return only an overall status string. The basic /health endpoint (returns status + incidents_count) is appropriate for public load-balancer checks; the detailed endpoint should require an internal or admin API key.

findings.title: Full ticket_json including PII is stored in rexus_analysis_log without data minimisation
findings.description: The analyze_ticket endpoint stores the complete raw ticket_json (line 696) and the full JSON response (line 706) into the rexus_analysis_log table. The ticket_json may contain: caller names (Caller field), phone numbers (Phone field), location data, company names, assigned_to names, and any other fields from the ServiceNow PDF or API response. The full_response likewise contains similar_incidents which includes caller_id and location from the incidents table per the SELECT at lines 617-626. The analytics endpoint at /analysis-log/{log_id} intentionally excludes these fields (SEC-004 fix), but the data is stored at rest in the database with no stated retention policy or encryption-at-rest controls.
findings.impact: The analysis log is an unbounded PII accumulator. Every analysis run persists a caller's personal data. If the database is compromised, or if a future endpoint inadvertently exposes input_json/full_response columns, PII leaks at scale. This is a GDPR/CCPA compliance risk.
findings.evidence: backend/api/routers/analyze.py:696 — json.dumps(req.ticket_json) stored as input_json. Line 706 — json.dumps(result) stored as full_response. The result dict at line 667 includes similar_incidents which contains caller_id and location (line 621-622 of the detail SELECT).
findings.recommendation: 1. Store only the sanitized cleaned_issue string instead of the raw ticket_json in input_json. 2. Strip PII fields (caller_id, location, company, assigned_to, phone) from similar_incidents before storing full_response. 3. Add a retention policy (e.g., auto-delete analysis logs older than 90 days via a scheduled job or pg_cron). 4. Document the PII data lifecycle in a privacy impact assessment.

findings.title: Prompt injection sanitization is incomplete — only covers a small regex allowlist of phrases
findings.description: _sanitize_for_prompt() uses a regex that matches a very narrow set of injection phrases: 'ignore previous instructions', 'system prompt', 'you are now', 'disregard'. Modern prompt injection attacks use many other vectors: instruction overrides in different languages, character substitution (ÌGNORE), base64-encoded instructions, indirect injection through resolution notes fetched from the database (evidence_text at line 411 which can be up to 3000 chars), and role-playing frames. Furthermore, only cleaned_issue and cluster_name are sanitized — the evidence_text itself (lines 311-313 in the playbook prompt) passes raw incident data including close_notes from the database into the LLM prompt without sanitization.
findings.impact: An attacker who can insert a crafted incident into the knowledge base (e.g., via /sync/import) could inject instructions into the evidence_text that get executed when a legitimate user triggers /analyze. The LLM could be made to return malicious content, exfiltrate data via crafted outputs, or bypass grounding rules.
findings.evidence: analyze.py:307-313 — evidence_text[:3000] is inserted directly into the playbook_prompt without sanitization. playbooks.py:358-410 — the same pattern with evidence_text from database incidents, also unsanitized.
findings.recommendation: 1. Apply _sanitize_for_prompt to evidence_text blocks, not just the user-supplied issue field. 2. Expand the injection pattern to cover: indirect prompt injection (e.g., '\n\nSystem:'), language variations, and common jailbreak phrases. 3. Consider using a structural prompt format that places evidence in a delimited block the LLM is instructed never to interpret as instructions. 4. Add output validation — if the LLM response contains suspicious content (e.g., instructions to the user, system-level text), reject or flag it.

findings.title: Missing Content-Security-Policy header — LLM-generated Markdown is rendered in the browser
findings.description: The security headers middleware adds X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, X-XSS-Protection, Referrer-Policy, and Cache-Control, but omits Content-Security-Policy (CSP). The frontend renders LLM-generated Markdown via react-markdown without rehype-raw, so raw HTML injection from LLM output is currently blocked by react-markdown's default behavior. However, without a CSP header, the browser has no fallback defense if a dependency update changes this behavior, or if future code adds dangerouslySetInnerHTML. The X-XSS-Protection header is also deprecated in modern browsers and provides no protection in Chrome/Firefox.
findings.impact: Low current risk due to react-markdown's safe defaults, but the absence of CSP removes defense-in-depth. Any future use of rehype-raw or dangerouslySetInnerHTML would immediately create XSS risk with no browser-level mitigation.
findings.evidence: backend/api/main.py:55-59 — headers added but no CSP. Analyze.tsx:452 — Markdown component used without rehype-raw (safe). package.json — react-markdown ^10.1.0 does not enable raw HTML by default.
findings.recommendation:
Add a Content-Security-Policy header in the security headers middleware. For an SPA with no inline scripts:

response.headers['Content-Security-Policy'] = (
    "default-src 'self'; "
    "script-src 'self'; "
    "style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; "
    "img-src 'self' data:; "
    "connect-src 'self';"
)

Also remove the deprecated X-XSS-Protection header.

findings.title: ServiceNow OAuth client_credentials grant has no TLS certificate verification enforcement
findings.description: All requests.get/post calls in ServiceNowClient do not pass verify=True explicitly. While requests defaults to True, there is no explicit enforcement or configuration option, and no check that the SERVICENOW_INSTANCE URL uses HTTPS. If SERVICENOW_INSTANCE is misconfigured to use HTTP, OAuth tokens and Bearer credentials would be transmitted in plaintext. Additionally there is no certificate pinning for the ServiceNow instance.
findings.impact: In misconfigured or development environments, OAuth tokens could be intercepted. The instance URL comes entirely from an environment variable with no scheme validation.
findings.evidence: servicenow_client.py:27 — instance_url = os.getenv('SERVICENOW_INSTANCE', '').rstrip('/') — no HTTPS scheme enforcement. Lines 45, 91, 106, 139, 162 — requests calls with no explicit verify= parameter.
findings.recommendation: 1. Add a startup check that SERVICENOW_INSTANCE starts with 'https://' and raise ValueError if it does not. 2. Pass verify=True explicitly on all requests calls. 3. Optionally support a SERVICENOW_CA_BUNDLE env var for corporate CA bundles: verify=os.getenv('SERVICENOW_CA_BUNDLE', True).

findings.title: feedback_type and input_method fields are not validated against an allowlist
findings.description: The FeedbackRequest model accepts feedback_type (max 50 chars) and input_method (max 20 chars) as free-text strings with only length constraints. These values are stored directly in the database. There is no validation that feedback_type is one of ['general', 'playbook', 'search', ...] or that input_method is one of ['text', 'voice']. This allows arbitrary string injection into the feedback table for these categorical fields.
findings.impact: While not directly exploitable for SQL injection (asyncpg uses parameterized queries), arbitrary values in categorical columns pollute analytics and reporting. An attacker can create confusion in the /feedback list endpoint by inserting misleading category values.
findings.evidence: feedback.py:21 — feedback_type: str = Field('general', max_length=50). No Literal type or enum constraint. Line 22 — input_method: str = Field('text', max_length=20). No allowlist.
findings.recommendation:
Use Python Literal types to constrain these fields:

from typing import Literal
feedback_type: Literal['general', 'playbook', 'search', 'accuracy', 'other'] = 'general'
input_method: Literal['text', 'voice'] = 'text'

findings.title: audio/mp3 content type allowed in transcription but saved with .webm suffix, enabling MIME type confusion
findings.description: The /transcribe endpoint accepts audio files with ALLOWED_AUDIO_TYPES = {audio/webm, audio/wav, audio/mp3, audio/mpeg, audio/ogg, audio/mp4} but always writes the temp file with a .webm suffix (line 85: suffix='.webm'). The content-type check only validates the HTTP Content-Type header, which the client fully controls. An attacker can upload a file with content_type='audio/webm' but actual content that is not audio. Additionally, the file extension check is bypassed if content_type is None (line 75: if file.content_type and ...) — when content_type is absent, any file type is accepted.
findings.impact: Malicious files could be passed to OpenAI Whisper. If Whisper or underlying audio processing libraries have parsing vulnerabilities (e.g., CVE in libav), a crafted audio file could exploit them. The None content-type bypass is the more significant issue.
findings.evidence: feedback.py:75 — if file.content_type and file.content_type not in ALLOWED_AUDIO_TYPES — the 'and' short-circuits when content_type is None, allowing all file types through. Line 85 — suffix='.webm' hardcoded regardless of actual content type.
findings.recommendation: 1. Validate magic bytes for allowed audio formats (e.g., WebM starts with 0x1A 0x45 0xDF 0xA3). 2. Remove the 'and' short-circuit — reject if content_type is missing: if not file.content_type or file.content_type not in ALLOWED_AUDIO_TYPES. 3. Use the correct file extension based on content_type.

findings.title: ticket_json field in AnalyzeRequest has no size or depth limit, enabling DoS via oversized payloads
findings.description: The AnalyzeRequest model defines ticket_json as a plain dict with no max_length, no depth validation, and no size constraint. An attacker can send a JSON payload with deeply nested structures (stack overflow), extremely large strings (memory exhaustion), or millions of keys. The rate limit of 20/minute applies at the IP level but a single large request can consume significant memory and CPU during embedding and LLM prompt construction. The build_embedding_text function concatenates multiple fields without individual field length limits beyond the 500-char sanitize truncation in _sanitize_for_prompt (which is only applied to two fields).
findings.impact: A single crafted large request can cause memory exhaustion or timeout during embedding. Combined with rate limiting gaps (the rate limit is checked after the request body is parsed), this creates a resource exhaustion vector.
findings.evidence: analyze.py:476 — ticket_json: dict — no validation constraints. The limiter at line 516 is applied after the request body is read and Pydantic parses the model. Max embedding text size is not bounded (build_embedding_text can produce arbitrarily large strings from unvalidated dict values).

findings.title: No structured security event logging for authentication failures, rate limit hits, or anomalous access patterns
findings.description: The application has no security-specific logging: rate limit violations are handled by slowapi's default handler without any log emission, there is no logging when endpoints are called with invalid formats (beyond FastAPI's default request logging), errors from the global exception handler are silently returned without log records, and there is no alerting mechanism for anomalous patterns (e.g., repeated 400s on /analyze suggesting probing). The existing logger.info/warning calls are purely business-logic level.
findings.impact: Incident detection is impossible. If an attacker is probing the API, brute-forcing parameters, or injecting prompts, there is no audit trail. Security investigations after a breach would have no forensic data beyond application-level transaction logs (which do not record IP addresses or user agents).
findings.evidence: main.py:64-66 — global_exception_handler returns 500 with no log statement. main.py:77 — slowapi rate limit handler emits no custom log. No middleware logs IP + endpoint + status for security monitoring.
findings.recommendation: 1. Add a request logging middleware that records: timestamp, IP, method, path, status code, response time. 2. Log rate limit violations at WARNING level with IP and endpoint. 3. In the global exception handler, log at ERROR level with traceback (server-side only). 4. Consider structured logging (JSON) to enable log aggregation and alerting.

findings.title: CORS default includes two development origins with credentials allowed — misconfiguration risk in production
findings.description: The CORS default is 'http://localhost:3000,http://localhost:5173' with allow_credentials=True. While this is overridable via CORS_ORIGINS, there is no validation that production deployments actually set this variable. If REXUS_ENV is set to 'production' but CORS_ORIGINS is not set, the API will respond to localhost-origin requests with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true — which is safe in that context (no cross-origin risk from localhost), but the pattern of silently defaulting to development values in production is a misconfiguration risk.
findings.impact: Low direct risk, but if a future change adds a wildcard or broader default, credentials could be exposed. The lack of a production-time validation check means CORS misconfiguration goes undetected.
findings.evidence: main.py:40 — os.getenv('CORS_ORIGINS', 'http://localhost:3000,http://localhost:5173') — no warning or error when production env lacks this setting. Line 45 — allow_credentials=True applies regardless.

findings.title: incidents endpoint exposes caller_id, location, and company without stated data classification
findings.description: The GET /incidents/{incident_number} endpoint returns caller_id, location, and company fields. These contain individual reporter identities (caller names, store locations). While work_notes are intentionally excluded (line 74 comment), the caller_id is still PII. The list endpoint GET /incidents does not return these fields, creating an inconsistency. There is no documentation indicating these fields should be restricted.
findings.impact: Internal users can enumerate all callers (customer service reps, store managers) who raised incidents. In a production deployment exposed beyond the internal network, this is a PII exposure.
findings.evidence: incidents.py:84 — caller_id, location, company included in the SELECT. These are not filtered out of the response.
findings.recommendation: Either: (1) remove caller_id, location, company from the default single-incident response and document they require a separate privileged endpoint, or (2) add field-level access control documentation clarifying this data is internal-only. At minimum, ensure the API is not publicly accessible without authentication (see SEC-001).

findings.title: Frontend dependencies use caret (^) version ranges — allows potentially vulnerable minor/patch upgrades
findings.description: All frontend dependencies use ^ (caret) ranges: react ^19.2.4, react-markdown ^10.1.0, lucide-react ^1.0.1, etc. The ^ range allows minor and patch version upgrades, which can include security fixes but also new vulnerabilities. Unlike the backend requirements.txt which pins exact versions (correctly), the frontend has no lock-file enforcement mechanism beyond package-lock.json. The react-markdown version ^10.1.0 is recent and does not enable dangerous HTML by default, but a future minor version change could alter behavior.
findings.impact: Low current risk since package-lock.json pins actual installed versions. Risk materialises if the lock file is deleted or regenerated (e.g., during CI/CD), allowing resolution to a vulnerable minor/patch version.
findings.evidence: frontend/package.json — all dependencies use ^ ranges. No .npmrc with exact=true. Backend requirements.txt correctly pins all exact versions with a comment documenting this (SEC-018).
findings.recommendation: Add .npmrc with 'save-exact=true' and 'package-lock=true' to prevent range resolution. Run 'npm audit' in CI/CD pipeline. Consider pinning exact versions in package.json to match the backend pattern.

findings.title: Hardcoded default INC number in frontend form pre-populates a real incident number
findings.description: The AnalyzePage component initializes incNumber state with 'INC2239275' — what appears to be a real production incident number. This means every new user who opens the Analyze page sees a specific incident number pre-filled. If this is a real incident, it could be fetched from ServiceNow inadvertently by users who click the Fetch button without understanding the pre-filled value.
findings.impact: Low technical security impact, but could cause accidental fetching and logging of a specific real incident's data in the analysis log. Also represents a placeholder that may contain sensitive context about the system's testing history.
findings.recommendation: Replace the hardcoded default with an empty string or a clearly fictional example: useState('') with placeholder='e.g. INC2061899' on the input element.

positive_findings: SQL injection: All database queries use asyncpg parameterized queries ($1, $2, ...) consistently across all routers — no string interpolation in SQL.
Secrets management: config.py enforces required env vars at startup with clear error messages and no hardcoded defaults for sensitive credentials.
Error handling: Global exception handler returns generic 'Internal server error' without leaking stack traces or internal details to clients.
Rate limiting: slowapi rate limiting applied to expensive endpoints (/analyze, /sync/import) with configurable limits.
CORS: Specific origin allowlist instead of wildcard (*), with credentials support limited to listed origins.
File upload validation: Both PDF and audio uploads validate content type, file size, and magic bytes (PDF) before processing.
Incident number validation: Regex validation on INC format applied on both /fetch-incident and /analyze/incident paths.
PII in API responses: work_notes explicitly excluded from similar_incidents response (line 664), analysis log GET endpoint excludes input_json and full_response (analytics.py:137).
Interactive API docs: Disabled in production via REXUS_ENV check (docs_url=None in production).
Security headers: X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, Cache-Control all set.
Dependency pinning: Backend requirements.txt uses exact version pins with documentation.
Token tracking: LLM API usage tracked per-endpoint for cost monitoring and anomaly detection.
Wave parameter validation: Allowlist regex enforced on wave path parameter to prevent path traversal.
Sort column injection: clusters.py uses Literal type for sort_by parameter preventing ORDER BY injection.
